facts.text: Geography studies the Earth's physical features, climates, environments, and human settlements.
The United States spans six time zones when Alaska and Hawaii are included.
The Grand Canyon in Arizona is one of the most famous natural landmarks in the United States.
The Mississippi River is one of the longest river systems in North America.
The Rocky Mountains stretch from Canada through the western United States.
The Great Lakes form the largest group of freshwater lakes on Earth by surface area.
Denali in Alaska is the highest peak in North America.
Yellowstone, established in 1872, became the world's first national park.
The Statue of Liberty, a gift from France, has welcomed arrivals to New York Harbor since 1886.
The Golden Gate Bridge is one of the most recognizable engineering landmarks in the United States.
Mount Rushmore features the carved faces of four U.S. presidents in South Dakota.
Geographers often divide the United States into major regions such as the Northeast, South, Midwest, West, and Pacific.
The U.S. includes deserts, wetlands, glaciers, plains, rainforests, coastlines, and volcanic landscapes all within one country.
Maps use scale, symbols, and projection systems to turn the curved Earth into flat images for study and navigation.
The Grand Canyon in Arizona was carved by the Colorado River and reveals layers of geological history.
Grand Canyon National Park is one of the most visited natural sites in the United States.
Grand Canyon is one of the most recognizable places or topics associated with Geography & USA Landmarks.
Travel guides about Geography & USA Landmarks frequently feature Grand Canyon.
Learning about Grand Canyon helps explain part of the geography, culture, or history of Geography & USA Landmarks.
For many visitors, Grand Canyon stands out as a major highlight of Geography & USA Landmarks.
Maps, documentaries, and guidebooks about Geography & USA Landmarks often highlight Grand Canyon.
The story of Grand Canyon is closely tied to how many people understand Geography & USA Landmarks.

The Equator divides Earth into the Northern and Southern Hemispheres and is one of the key reference lines in geography.
Many world geography lessons begin with the Equator because it helps explain climate zones and latitude.
Equator is one of the first topics that comes up when people explore Geography.
Learning about Equator gives a stronger feel for the depth and variety inside Geography.
Books, documentaries, and reference guides on Geography often highlight Equator.
Equator remains one of the most widely recognized examples connected with Geography.
For many readers, Equator is a memorable entry point into Geography.
The story of Equator helps explain why Geography stays so interesting to learn about.
Equator is frequently mentioned in discussions, lessons, and articles about Geography.
One reason Geography feels so rich is that it includes major touchpoints like Equator.
Readers interested in Geography often come across Equator very quickly.
Equator still stands out as a classic reference point within Geography.
